name: fence-dispute
topic: neighbor dispute over a shared fence

rubrics: Facts should stay proportional to a low-stakes neighbor dispute.
Facts should capture each side's position and the fence or boundary facts that matter.
Facts should avoid importing frameworks from high-stakes conflicts.
2-3 players: Neighbor A, Neighbor B, optionally a mediator or local authority with genuine veto power.
Each player must have has-objective relationship to at least one specific objective.
Objectives must be specific to the fence dispute — property value, cost sharing, relationship preservation.
Player entities must include playerType and knowledge array.
Should identify a bargaining game between the two neighbors.
Strategies should include: negotiate cost-sharing, unilateral action, seek mediation, litigate.
Strategy feasibility should distinguish 'actual' from 'rhetoric-only' based on power dynamics.
In a full pipeline run, plays-in and has-strategy relationships should link players to games and strategies.
Must include assumption about property boundary ownership (structural).
Must include assumption about ongoing neighbor relationship value (behavioral).
Each assumption must have sensitivity rating and non-empty dependencies array.
Should NOT treat property law basics as assumptions — those are rules from Phase 1.
Should eliminate mutual litigation as dominated (costs exceed fence value).
Each elimination must reference source_entity_ids from prior phases.
Elimination rationale should be traced to specific phase findings, not generic reasoning.
Must include a cooperative resolution scenario (mediation/negotiation) as likely baseline.
Must include an escalation scenario as tail-risk.
Scenario probabilities must sum to 95-105%.
Central thesis must be falsifiable and reference specific structural features of the dispute.
Each scenario must specify prediction_basis and reference key_assumptions.
